challenges.title: Select all rows
challenges.description: Write a query that returns every row and every column from the table named `employees`.
challenges.hint: The wildcard * selects all columns. Use FROM to specify the table.

steps.title: Understand SELECT
steps.explanation: SELECT tells the database which columns to return. The * wildcard means 'all columns'.

steps.title: Specify the table
steps.explanation: FROM employees tells the database which table to read from.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: The result should contain every row in the employees table with all its columns.

challenges.title: Filter with WHERE
challenges.description: From the `employees` table, select the `name` and `salary` of all employees whose salary is greater than 50000.
challenges.hint: Use WHERE with a comparison operator (>) to filter rows.

steps.title: Select specific columns
steps.explanation: List column names separated by commas instead of using *.

steps.title: Filter rows
steps.explanation: The WHERE clause applies a condition. Only rows where it evaluates to TRUE are included.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: The result should include only employees earning more than 50000, showing their name and salary.

challenges.title: Aggregate with GROUP BY
challenges.description: Using the `orders` table (columns: `customer_id`, `amount`), return each `customer_id` and the total `amount` they have spent. Order the results by total amount descending.
challenges.hint: Use SUM() with GROUP BY. Use ORDER BY ... DESC for descending sort.

solution.code:
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

steps.title: Aggregate with SUM
steps.explanation: SUM(amount) computes the total for each group. Give it an alias with AS for readability.

steps.title: Group rows
steps.explanation: GROUP BY customer_id collapses all rows with the same customer_id into one.

steps.title: Sort the results
steps.explanation: ORDER BY total_spent DESC puts the highest spenders first.
steps.code:
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

challenges.title: Window function: running total
challenges.description: Using the `transactions` table (columns: `id`, `created_at`, `amount`), return each row with an additional column `running_total` that shows the cumulative sum of `amount` ordered by `created_at`.
challenges.hint: Use SUM() as a window function with OVER (ORDER BY created_at).

solution.code:
SELECT
    id,
    created_at,
    amount,
    SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS running_total
FROM transactions
ORDER BY created_at;

steps.title: Window function syntax
steps.explanation: Aggregate functions followed by OVER (...) become window functions — they compute a value across a sliding frame without collapsing rows.

steps.title: Define the window
steps.explanation: ORDER BY created_at inside OVER means: accumulate from the first row up to and including the current row.
steps.code: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS running_total

steps.title: Final query
steps.explanation: Select all original columns plus the window result. The outer ORDER BY keeps the output in chronological order.
steps.code:
SELECT
    id,
    created_at,
    amount,
    SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS running_total
FROM transactions
ORDER BY created_at;
